2604.07964.title: Are we still able to recognize pearls? Machine-driven peer review and the risk to creativity: An explainable RAG-XAI detection framework with markers extraction
2604.07964.paper_summary: The integration of large language models (LLMs) into peer review raises a concern beyond authorship and detection: the potential cascading automation of the entire editorial process. As reviews become partially or fully machine-generated, it becomes plausible that editorial decisions may also be delegated to algorithmic systems, leading to a fully automated evaluation pipeline. They risk reshaping the criteria by which scientific work is assessed. This paper argues that machine-driven assessment may systematically favor standardized, pattern-conforming research while penalizing unconventional and paradigm-shifting ideas that require contextual human judgment. We consider that this shift could lead to epistemic homogenization, where researchers are implicitly incentivized to optimize their work for algorithmic approval rather than genuine discovery. To address this risk, we introduce an explainable framework (RAG-XAI) for assessing review quality and detecting automated patterns using markers LLM extractor, aiming to preserve transparency, accountability and creativity in science. The proposed framework achieves near-perfect detection performance, with XGBoost, Random Forest and LightGBM reaching 99.61% accuracy, AUC-ROC above 0.999 and F1-scores of 0.9925 on the test set, while maintaining extremely low false positive rates (<0.23%) and false negative rates (~0.8%). In contrast, the logistic regression baseline performs substantially worse (89.97% accuracy, F1-score 0.8314). Feature importance and SHAP analyses identify absence of personal signals and repetition patterns as the dominant predictors. Additionally, the RAG component achieves 90.5% top-1 retrieval accuracy, with strong same-class clustering in the embedding space, further supporting the reliability of the framework's outputs.
2604.07964.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型（LLMs）整合進同行評審中引發了一個超越作者身份和檢測的擔憂：整個編輯過程潛在的級聯自動化。<lb/>隨著評審部分或完全由機器生成，編輯決策也可能被委託給算法系統，導致完全自動化的評估管道。<lb/>這些風險可能重塑科學工作評估的標準。<lb/>本文主張，機器驅動的評估可能系統性地偏向標準化、符合模式的研究，同時懲罰需要上下文人類判斷的非常規和範式轉變的想法。<lb/>我們認為，這一轉變可能導致認識論的同質化，研究人員在潛意識中被激勵去優化他們的工作以獲得算法的認可，而非真正的發現。<lb/>為了應對這一風險，我們引入了一個可解釋的框架（RAG-XAI）來評估評審質量並使用標記的LLM提取器檢測自動化模式，旨在保持科學中的透明度、問責性和創造力。<lb/>所提出的框架達到了近乎完美的檢測性能，XGBoost、隨機森林和LightGBM在測試集上達到了99.61%的準確率，AUC-ROC超過0.999，F1分數為0.9925，同時保持極低的假陽性率（<0.23%）和假陰性率（約0.8%）。<lb/>相比之下，邏輯回歸基線的表現明顯較差（89.97%的準確率，F1分數0.8314）。<lb/>特徵重要性和SHAP分析確定了個人信號缺失和重複模式作為主要預測因子。<lb/>此外，RAG組件在嵌入空間中達到了90.5%的頂級檢索準確率，並在同類聚類中表現出強大的聚合性，進一步支持了該框架輸出的可靠性。
2604.07964.author: Alin-Gabriel Văduva et.al.
2604.07964.authors: Alin-Gabriel Văduva, Simona-Vasilica Oprea, Adela Bâra
